content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 12, 2017

Rosann Gallien
Assistant General Counsel
San Diego County Water Authority,
4677 Overland Avenue
San Diego, California 92123-1233

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-145

Dear Ms. Gallien:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Jerry Butkiewicz, Board member
of the San Diego County Water Authority ("Water Authority"), regarding the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090, et seq.¹ Please note that we do
not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of
interest. Nor do we advise under other areas of law that could be affected, including restrictions on
incompatible activities. We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we forwarded your request and all pertinent facts
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County District
Attorney's Office, as required by state law. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written
response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) The following advice is not admissible in a
criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor. (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Under the Act, may Mr. Butkiewicz participate in Water Authority decisions involving
employee representative group, Teamsters Local 911, where he is a volunteer to the San Diego-
Imperial Counties Labor Council ("Labor Council")?

2. May he participate if he receives remuneration either in the form of a per diem and travel
reimbursements or as an employee with a monthly salary from the Labor Council?

3. Is he prohibited from participating in these decisions under Section 1090 as a volunteer or
if he receives remuneration from the Labor Council?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 5 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-145
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes, as a volunteer, Mr. Butkiewicz currently does not have a financial interest in the
Labor Council such that he would be prohibited from participating under the Act.

2. Yes, while he would have a financial interest in the Labor Council, because the Teamsters
Local 911 pays dues that are less than approximately one half of one percent of the overall Labor
Council funding and no facts indicate these dues would change or that these decisions would affect
Mr. Butkiewicz's remuneration from the Labor Council, there is not a prohibited conflict of interest.

3. No, he is not prohibited from participating in these decisions under Section 1090 as a
volunteer or if he receives remuneration from the Labor Council, as discussed below.

FACTS

Jerry Butkiewicz is a City of San Diego representative on the Water Authority's Board of
Directors. He is the retired secretary-treasurer of the San Diego-Imperial Counties Labor Council
("Labor Council") and currently volunteers his services at the Labor Council without any
remuneration. Mr. Butkiewicz has been offered part-time employment with the organization for a
monthly salary in the range of $2,000 and $2,500. Based on the questions in the request, it appears
that he may be offered per diem and travel reimbursements in the future.

The Labor Council is a Section 501(c)(5) entity under the Internal Revenue Code. It is the
local central body affiliate of the AFL-CIO and offers an avenue for the local unions to come
together as a unified group. It is considered the "union of unions". The AFL-CIO has recently
planted the Labor Council in receivership and the Council is seeking to reorganize. The Labor
Council is funded by union dues. It is made up of approximately 120 local unions, and each affiliate
union member contributes to the Labor Council.

The Water Authority's employee representative group is the Teamsters Local 911 of the
International Brotherhood of Teamsters and a member of the Labor Council. Teamsters Local 911
represents various Water Authority employee groups including Technical Support Group,
Professional Administrative Group, and Managerial Supervisory Group. Its purpose is to advocate
terms and conditions of employment for its members, and it is primarily funded through collection
of union dues paid by its members. As a member of the Labor Council, Teamsters Local 911
contributes union dues to the Council. Mr. Butkiewicz believes that Teamsters Local 911
contributes only a small portion of the Labor Council dues, in the amount of approximately one half
of one percent of the overall Labor Council funding.

The Water Authority has a current Memorandum of Understanding with the Teamsters
Local 911, effective July 1, 2015 through June 30, 2019. The next union negotiations under the
Meyers-Milias-Brown Act² will occur early in 2019. The Water Authority's Board of Directors

² The Meyers-Milias Brown Act governs labor management relations in government employment fields and
serves to promote full communication between public agencies and their employees. The Act requires government

File No. A-17-145
Page No. 3

appoints its negotiators to conduct union negotiations, and must approve the MOU before it can go
into effect.

ANALYSIS

Conflict of Interest under the Act

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's
interests. (Section 87103.) Section 87103 of the Act lists several types of financial interests that can
give rise to a conflict of interest. The financial interests most relevant to the facts in this situation
are:

• A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more
(Section 87103(a); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Any source of income, such as a business entity, from which the official has received
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. This also includes income
from any client of the business entity of at least $500, provided to and received by the public
official within 12 months before the decision is made.

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family.

Because Section 82005 defines a "business entity" as any organization or enterprise
operated for profit, the Labor Council, as a nonprofit entity, is not a "business entity" under the Act.

Under his current arrangement as a volunteer, Mr. Butkiewicz does not have a financial
interest in the Labor Council under the Act. However, in the event he accepted payments from the
Labor Council as salary, or per diem and travel reimbursements, Mr. Butkiewicz would have a
financial interest in the Labor Council as a source of income once he met the threshold amounts.
Income is defined under the Act to include reimbursement of expenses, and any per diem. (Section
82030(a).)

He will also have a financial interest in any dues-paying union to the Labor Council that
results in $500 being provided to him within twelve months of a decision. And he will have a
financial interest in his personal finances. We note that where the Teamsters Local 911 pays less
than half of one percent of the Labor Council's funds, it is not likely that the Teamsters Local 911

employers to meet and confer in good-faith with labor unions and representatives regarding wages, hours, and terms or
conditions of employment. (See Govt. Code Section 3500, et seq.)

File No. A-17-145
Page No. 4

will meet the financial threshold to qualify as a "source of income" to him. We examine whether
Mr. Bufkiewicz may participate in Water Authority decisions involving employee representative
group, Teamsters Local 911 of the International Brotherhood of Teamsters.

1. Volunteer to the Labor Council

As a volunteer to the Labor Counsel with no remuneration, Mr. Bufkiewicz does not have a
conflict of interest under the Act, and may participate in these decisions as a member of the Water
Authority Board of Directors.

2. Receiving remuneration from the Labor Counsel.

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material.

Foreseeability and Materiality: The standard for foreseeability differs depending on
whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. A financial effect on a financial interest is
presumed "reasonably foreseeable" if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision as a named
party in, or subject of, the proceeding. (Regulation 18701(a).) Here, it is not anticipated from the facts
that the Labor Council will be a named party or subject to these labor contract decisions. For
decisions involving a source of income not named or subject to the proceeding, Regulation
18701(b) states that a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it is a realistic possibility and
more than hypothetical. As to materiality, Regulation 18702.3(a)(3) states that where the source of
income is a nonprofit the standard is whether the nonprofit will receive a measurable financial
benefit or loss due to the decision. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).)

Under the facts provided it does not appear likely or realistic that Mr. Bufkiewicz's
remuneration from the Labor Council would alter in any way as the result of negotiations between
the Water Authority and the Teamsters Local 911. It is unlikely that any decision regarding the
Teamsters Local 911 would increase or decrease the amount of Labor Council dues it pays, and
then result in the Labor Council experiencing a measurable financial gain or loss. Such a financial
effect is not reasonably foreseeable. Therefore, Mr. Bufkiewicz will not have a conflict of interest
under the Act.

Section 1090

The analysis of a financial interest under Section 1090 is separate and distinct from that
applied under the Act. Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official
capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition applies
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call
(1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646). Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or
minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided
allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. A contract that violates Section 1090 is
void. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.)

File No. A-17-145
Page No. 5

As a general rule, when Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a
public entity, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the
entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. However, the Legislature has
created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the financial interest
involved is deemed a "remote interest" and involves a public body or board as defined in Section
1091, or is deemed a "noninterest" as defined in Section 1091.5.

Where a remote interest is present, the contract may be lawfully executed provided: (1) the
officer discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency; (2) the interest is
noted in the public body's official records; and (3) the officer completely abstains from any
participation in the making of the contract. (Section 1091.)

We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict
of interest under Section 1090. These steps are: (1) does the matter involve a public officer or
employee; (2) involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in making a contract;
(4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or non-interest
exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, (6) does the rule of necessity apply?

Steps one through three are not at issue here. This matter involves a public officer, contract
labor negotiations, which Mr. Butkiewicz wishes to participate in as a member of the Water
Authority. Our focus is on step four.

Step Four: Does the Official Have a Financial Interest in Contracts with Teamster Local
911 as a volunteer or if he receives remuneration from the Labor Council?

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest" (People v. Honig, (1996) 48 Cal. App. 4th 289, 333), and officials are deemed to
have a financial interest in a contract if they might profit from it in any way. (Id.) Although Section
1090 nowhere specifically defines the term "financial interest," case law and Attorney General
Opinions state that prohibited financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve
financial losses, or the possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. (Thomson,
supra, at pp. 645, 651-652; see also People v. Vallerga (1977) 67 Cal.App.3d 847, 867, fn. 5; Terry
v. Bender (1956) 143 Cal.App.2d 198, 207-208; People v. Darby (1952) 114 Cal.App.2d 412, 431-
432; 85 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36-38 (2002). Employees have been found to have a financial
interest in a contract that involves their employer, even where the contract would not result in a
change in income or directly involve the employee, because the employee has an overall interest in
the financial success of the firm and continued employment. (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 161-162
(2001).)

As a volunteer, Mr. Butkiewicz does not have a financial interest at issue for purposes of
Section 1090. The question is whether he might have a financial interest as the recipient of
remunerations from the Labor Council in the contract negotiations with one of the Council's
member unions. Based on the facts provided, we do not find evidence that he would experience any
change in his financial circumstances, nor in that of his employer's status, nor his continued
employment related to the decisions at issue. Absent such facts, it appears that Mr. Butkiewicz does

File No. A-17-145
Page No. 6

not have a financial interest in decisions regarding the Teamsters Local 911 before the Water
Authority related to his employment with the Labor Council. No further analysis is required.

Please contact our office if a specific decision is under consideration involving different
facts.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division
